CARD: "Kinematics describes how objects move without reference to force. Temperature is a scalar, but acceleration is a vector quantity."
VERDICT: score_10=9, understandable=true, error=false, all gates true, failure_reasons=[]. Reasoning: "The clip defines kinematics and distinguishes between scalar and vector quantities, providing clear examples for each. It introduces concepts like distance, displacement, speed, and velocity, explaining their differences and relationships. The definitions are complete and well-illustrated, making the clip understandable on its own."
FULL TEXT: "in this video we're going to talk about kinematics which basically describes how objects move without any references to force now we're going to focus on kinematics in one dimension mostly along the x-axis but we can also work on some problems along the y-axis when you get into two-dimensional kinematics it covers projectile motion which is a topic for another day now the first thing we need to talk about is the difference between a scalar quantity and a vector quantity a scalar quantity is something that has magnitude only whereas the vector quantity has both magnitude and direction so for instance mass is a scalar quantity you could say that a book has two kilograms of mass the two kilograms would be the magnitude of the mass of the book but you wouldn't say the book has two kilograms of mass east direction wouldn't apply for mass so mass cannot be a vector quantity distance is a scalar quantity and displacement is a vector quantity displacement you can think of it as distance with direction so let's say if a person traveled 15 meters you're basically describing the person's distance because you didn't mention direction but let's say if someone traveled 15 meters east now you're describing displacement you describe in not only how far he traveled but also where he traveled so think of displacement as distance with direction now technically displacement is really the change in the position of an object so along the x-axis you could describe displacement as the final position minus the initial position speed is a scalar quantity speed describes how fast an object is moving velocity is a vector quantity velocity is speed with direction so if you were to say a person is traveling at uh 20 meters per second you're describing the speed of the person this is the magnitude but let's say if a person is moving that 30 meters per second north you're describing velocity because you've mentioned his speed along with the direction so you have both magnitude and direction which makes it a vector quantity"

meters per second you're describing the speed of the person this is the magnitude but let's say if a person is moving that 30 meters per second north you're describing velocity because you've mentioned his speed along with the direction so you have both magnitude and direction which makes it a vector quantity now what about temperature would you say that temperature is a scalar quantity or would you describe it as a vector quantity so think of units of temperature like let's say it's uh 80 degrees fahrenheit outside or 25 degrees celsius you wouldn't say it's 80 degrees fahrenheit north direction doesn't apply for temperature so because you can't describe the direction of temperature it's not relevant temperature is a scalar quantity you can only mention its magnitude such as how hot it is 80 degrees fahrenheit but you can attach direction to temperature which makes it scalar now acceleration that is a vector quantity acceleration tells you how fast the velocity is changing and you could say a car is accelerating at two meters per second squared east you can put direction to it so acceleration is the vector quantity now let's talk more about the difference between distance given a situation calculate the distance and the displacement of this individual that uh traveled here let's put a person so the total distance that this person traveled is 17 meters it's basically the sum of these two numbers he traveled 13 meters east and then 4 meters west so a total distance of 17 meters distance being scalar it's always positive now there is an exception to this FULL TEXT: "final position is equal to initial position plus v initial t plus one half a t squared now because this is x we're dealing with motion along the x axis but when you go into projectile motion you can apply motion along the y axis as well so you might see this variation of this formula y final equals y initial plus v y initial t so that's initial velocity but in the y direction plus one half a t squared where a is like the gravitational acceleration in the y direction so just understand that you can apply these formulas in the x direction or in the y direction so it might be a lot to keep track of but as you begin to work problems uh the use of these formulas will begin to make more sense but you may want to write these down for your reference now before we work on a few example problems there's something i do want to mention when dealing with constant speed you could use this formula x final is equal to x initial plus v t so you don't need to write v bar because v and v bar are the same uh when dealing with constant speed but for constant acceleration it's good to keep in mind that this represents average velocity so it's better to write v bar instead of v so there's no confusion because these two they're not necessarily the same when dealing with constant acceleration so just be aware of that little detail let's start with this one a bus is traveling at a constant speed of 40 meters per second how many hours will it take to travel a distance of 200 miles so what formula do we need to use so we're given a speed which we can use as a symbol v or s and we know the distance which is 200 miles the only formula that we could use since the bus is moving at constant speed is this one d is equal to vt now before we use that formula we need to take a look at the units here we have meters per second and here we have mouse and we want to find a time in hours so we're looking for t the units they don't match so before we can use the formula we need to convert the units into an appropriate form so what do you recommend that we need to do we have the distance in mouse and we want to find the time in hours the best thing we can do is convert the speed from meters per second to miles per hour once we do that then it will match with the unit hours and the unit miles so we can now use that formula so let's go ahead and convert 40 meters per second to miles per hour so let's get an overview of how we're going to do this let's convert meters to kilometers and then kilometers to miles and then we're going to convert seconds to minutes and then minutes to hours"

VERDICT: score_10=4, understandable=false, error=false; failed gates: purpose_identifiable, problem_statement_complete. failure_reasons: [missing_problem_statement: "The clip does not explicitly state the problem being solved, only the context card mentions an initial speed and the need for unit conversion."]. Reasoning: "...the problem statement itself (what is being asked to find) is not explicitly stated within the clip... the clip jumps directly into unit conversions without clearly defining the initial problem."
FULL TEXT: "let's convert seconds to minutes since we have seconds on the bottom we want to put seconds on top and then minutes on the bottom one minute is equal to 60 seconds so now we can cross out this unit and now let's convert minutes to hours one hour is equivalent to 60 minutes and so we could cancel the unit minutes so now let's do the math we're going to multiply by all the numbers that are on the top but we're going to divide by the numbers on the bottom so it's going to be 40 divided by a thousand take that result divided by 1.609 and then multiply that by 60 and then by another 60. so for the velocity or rather the speed you should get 89.5 if you round it miles per hour which we can write mph so that's how we can convert meters per second to miles per hour now let's go ahead and finish this problem so let's use the formula d is equal to vt in this case d is going to represent the distance which is 200 miles v is going to be the speed which is 89.5 miles per hour and then now we can calculate t so to get t by itself we need to divide both sides by 89.5 miles per hour 200 divided by 89.5 gives us this answer 2.23 hours now looking at the units we can see that the unit miles cancel leaving behind the unit hours which is what we want the final answer to be in so when dealing with these problems always check to make sure that the units match if they don't match you need to convert one unit into another so just keep that in mind"

my_assessment:
METRICS ROW (step 4, mode=rebuild): {"domain": "lecture", "topic": "(none)", "n_clips": 4, "has_perception": true, "comprehension_rate": 0.25, "mean_judge_score": 0.525, "judge_error_rate": 0.0, "ends_on_period_rate": 1.0, "unresolved_reference_rate": 1.0, "context_complete_rate": 1.0, "prerequisite_gap_rate": 1.0, "worked_example_completeness": 0.0, "visual_completeness": 1.0, "sequence_coherence": 1.0, "carded_rate": 1.0, "grounding_precision": 1.0, "rejections_repair": 5, "rejections_snap": 0, "rejections_dedupe": 2, "rejections_post_merge_judge": 0, "rejections_quality_floor": 0, "rejections_max_clips": 0, "n_merged": 1} [rebuilt].

PER-CLIP HUMAN JUDGMENT vs the completeness contracts:
- Clip 1 (definition, 1.76-187.76): PASSES its contract. Concepts named + defined (kinematics, scalar/vector, distance/displacement, speed/velocity), equation given (displacement = final position - initial position), interpretation via examples, no unresolved this/that, starts at the video's opening and ends on a completed definition. A learner watching only this clip is fine. One defect: the card's second sentence ("Temperature is a scalar, but acceleration is a vector quantity") is grounded in unit u0008 but describes content NOT inside the clip — a misleading preview, though not a hallucination. The judge's 9/10 matches my read. This is the one genuinely shippable clip.
- Clip 2 (claim, merged, 143.12-373.84): FAILS. It explicitly poses "calculate the distance and the displacement of this individual", answers only the distance (17 m), and is cut BEFORE the displacement answer (+9 m lives at 399.8-446.0, outside the span); it then wanders into the negative-temperature tangent and ends semantically mid-sentence ("some places can be negative 20" — "degrees fahrenheit" is the next sentence). ~45 s (sentence 5) duplicates clip 1's ending. The ASR inconsistency "30 meters east" vs "he traveled 13 meters east" is left unexplained in-clip. The card is wrong in framing: it says "Earlier, the speaker defined acceleration as a vector quantity" when that definition is INSIDE the clip. Pipeline shipped it at final_quality 0.95/completeness 1.0 because the anchor role is "claim", so required_verdict_fields (base.py:198-204) excludes the problem_* gates that the merged span actually fails — the judge's 4/10 (missing_result) is right and the internal score is wrong. Learner outcome: promised two answers, given one, ends mid-thought.
- Clip 3 (claim, 1252.96-1487.32): FAILS. Starts mid-derivation ("final position is equal to..." is the tail of the u0039->u0040 substitution), repeatedly points at unseen board writing ("this formula", "write these down"), then states the bus problem (40 m/s, how many hours for 200 miles — a complete problem statement) and ends on the conversion PLAN, before any solving. It is a problem-statement clip welded to an equations tail, ending on a cliffhanger. Judge 4/10 is fair; pipeline shipped it at 0.85 because "claim" again bypasses the problem gates.
- Clip 4 (result, 1529.84-1688.96): FAILS the worked-example contract. The question ("how many hours will it take to travel a distance of 200 miles") is never stated in-clip and the card does not state it either; the clip starts mid-conversion-chain ("let's convert seconds to minutes...") so the arithmetic "40 divided by a thousand ... divided by 1.609" references meters->km->miles steps the viewer never saw. Givens partially present (d=200 miles, v=89.5 mph), equation (d=vt), substitution, result (t=2.23 hours), units check and interpretation present. completeness 0.475 and judge 4/10 (missing_problem_statement, purpose_identifiable=false) are consistent with my read. Critically, clips 3+4 are the two halves of ONE worked example severed at 1487.3/1529.8 — and the 42 s between them (1487.5-1529.8: the meters->km->miles conversion steps) landed in NEITHER clip; prerequisite_clips=[] on clip 4, so nothing even tells the learner to watch clip 3 first.

REJECTIONS — wrongly vs rightly killed:
- c_u0060 (bus part b, repair): WRONGLY KILLED — the clearest yield loss. The 1693.5-1885.4 span is a nearly ideal, self-contained worked example: problem stated in its FIRST sentence, givens (xi=+50 mi, xf=-90 mi, t=5 h), sign reasoning, formula (v-bar = (xf-xi)/t), substitution (-140/5), result (-28 mph) with units and interpretation. The judge's missing_problem_statement kind is factually false for this span; "part b of this problem" is the only dangling phrase and is harmless. This was exactly the reference-outcome "worked example" clip and the repair stage killed it at score 0.5.
- c_u0035 / c_u0040 (equations block, repair): yield problem. Every equation in the span is spoken aloud in full; the dangling bits are board-pointing ("write that equation here"). Killing BOTH means the lecture's kinematic-equation list — the reference "equations" clip — ships nowhere except accidentally as clip 3's mid-thought head. Marginally defensible individually, collectively a coverage failure.
- c_u0019 (repair): rightly killed AS BUILT (span ends mid-solution before the -10 m/s result), but it exposes an upstream defect: the average-speed/velocity particle example (554-817s, a complete worked example in the video) has NO unit labeled result/solution (u0023/u0024 are "calculation", u0025 "worked_step" — calculation is not an anchor role in the lecture adapter, lecture.py:39-41), so no anchor could ever capture it, and repair's pull_result found only u0054 (866 s away, beyond closure_max_span_s=300). Role-labeling error -> whole worked example unclippable.
- c_u0030 (repair): rightly killed — a 19-second fragment whose content reappears in clip 3.
- dedupe x2 (c_u0003, c_u0046): rightly killed, true duplicates of surviving spans.

COVERAGE vs the ~5-clip reference outcome (definition, equations, worked example, practice problem, practice solution): delivered ~1 of 5. (a) Definitions: YES — clip 1 is good. (b) Equations: NO — both derivation candidates killed at repair; equation_introduction units u0033/u0039 never got anchor slots. (c) Worked examples: NONE complete — the video contains FOUR (distance/displacement 13E/4W at 269-531; avg speed/velocity particle at 554-817; bus part a at 1373-1689; bus part b at 1693-1885). Part a shipped severed across clips 3/4 with a 42 s hole and no prerequisite link; the distance/displacement example shipped truncated before its second answer inside clip 2; the particle example was never anchorable (role labeling); part b was wrongly rejected. worked_example_completeness=0.0 in the pipeline's own metrics agrees. (d/e) Practice problem + linked solution: NO — the lecture has a textbook practice moment (u0006 practice_prompt "is temperature scalar or vector?" + u0007/u0008 answer) but practice_prompt (priority 52) lost the MAX_ANCHORS=12 race to six priority-70 "claim" units and thirteen priority-68 definitions (candidates.py:76-90); no practice clip, no solution clip, no linkage. Also u0007/u0008 are labeled claim/explanation rather than solution, so even with a slot the practice->solution pairing could not have formed.

SYSTEMIC FINDINGS: (1) Internal quality scores and the clip-only judge diverge wildly (final_quality 0.77-0.97 vs judge 4/10 on 3 of 4 clips) because completeness_score keys off required_verdict_fields for the ANCHOR role — "claim" anchors skip problem_statement/reasoning/result gates even when the assembled span contains a worked problem. (2) The repair loop kills near-perfect worked examples (part b) on dubious judge failure kinds while the quality floor passes mid-thought fragments. (3) Anchor selection has no role/chapter diversity: flat priority ordering + cap 12 crowds out practice_prompt, solution, equation_introduction entirely. (4) Same-facet merge (clip 2) can weld a definition run to half a worked example and then re-score it against the weaker role's gates. (5) unresolved_reference_rate=1.0 and prerequisite_gap_rate=1.0 across shipped clips: every clip assumes at least one concept not introduced in-clip or card. (6) Boundary snap ends_on_period=1.0 is nominal only — clip 2 ends on a sentence-index boundary that is semantically mid-sentence ("negative 20"). Compared to the previous run's single 12 s clip, the structure-first pipeline is a large improvement in structure quality (61 well-labeled units, sane content map) and clip count (4), but by its own headline metric only 25% of shipped clips are self-contained, and the flagship lecture contracts (worked example, practice pair, equations) are essentially unmet.
verdict_one_line: Pipeline now ships 4 clips (was 1) from the kinematics lecture, but only the definitions clip (judge 9/10) meets its contract — comprehension_rate 0.25, worked_example_completeness 0.0, the near-perfect bus-part-b worked example was wrongly rejected at repair, the equations block died at repair, and the practice-prompt/solution pair was crowded out of the 12-anchor cap, so roughly 1 of the 5 reference outcomes is delivered.
